result.markdown:
--- engine: knitr title: Process other response types --- # ️✅ Learning objectives ::: nonincremental - Parse text responses. - Parse binary responses such as images and videos. - Handle HTTP error responses. ::: ::: {.cell} ::: ::: notes - "After we finish this chapter, we'll be able to..." (objectives) - Definitely would like feedback about depth of coverage! ::: # Content types - `Content-Type` header aka "MIME type" - "Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions" - `type/subtype;parameter=value` - `httr2::resp_content_type()` gets `type/subtype` - `httr2::resp_encoding()` gets `charset` parameter - More at [MDN MIME types](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Basics_of_HTTP/MIME_types) - Even more at [IANA Media Types registry](https://www.iana.org/assignments/media-types/media-types.xhtml) ::: notes - type/subtype tells you which function to use to parse it - Could have typos, etc! - Encoding: If you're getting weird characters, incorrect character set could be the problem. - IANA = Internet Assigned Numbers Authority ::: # Text content types |MIME type |httr2 function |Description | |------------------|--------------------|------------------------------| |`application/json`|`resp_body_json()` |By far most common | |`application/xml` |`resp_body_xml()` |Briefly most common | |`text/html` |`resp_body_html()` |Really a subclass of xml | |`text/plain` |`resp_body_string()`|Text wildcard | ::: notes - We'll see on later slides that sometimes people use alternative strings ::: ## JSON responses - `application/json` or `*/json` - `httr2::resp_body_json()` uses `jsonlite::fromJSON()` ::: notes this slide & next might be entirely covered in earlier chapter ::: ## JSON data - 4 scalars (length-1 vectors) - `null` ≈ `NA` - `string` ≈ `character(1)`, always `"` (not `'`) - `number` ≈ `numeric(1)`, no `Inf`/`-Inf`/`NaN` - `boolean` ≈ `logical(1)`, `true` = `TRUE`, `false` = `FALSE` - `array` ≈ unnamed `list()` - `[]`: `[null, "a", 1, true]` ≈ `list(NULL, "a", 1, TRUE)` - `object` ≈ named `list()` - `{}`: `{"a": 1, "b": [1, 2]}` ≈
`list(a = 1, b = list(1, 2))` ::: notes - We never went into details about what's legal in the JSON format. - Arrays of the same type are roughly equivalent to vectors, parsers like jsonlite often turn such arrays into vectors. ::: ## XML responses e**X**tensible **M**arkup **L**anguage - `application/xml`, `text/xml`, or `*/xml` - `httr2::resp_body_xml()` uses `xml2::read_xml()` ## XML data - Tags as `contents` - Everything nestable ## XML example: raw ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} resp_xml <- req_template(request(example_url()), "/xml") |> req_perform() resp_xml |> resp_body_string() |> cat() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` #> #> #>
#> New York #> 10021-3100 #> NY #> 21 2nd Street #>
#> 27 #> #> John #> true #> Smith #> #> #> 212 555-1234 #> home #> #> #> 646 555-4567 #> office #> #> #> #> ``` ::: ::: ## XML example: parsed ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} extracted_xml <- resp_body_xml(resp_xml) class(extracted_xml) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` #> [1] "xml_document" "xml_node" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # We'll see other ways to parse this in {rvest} chapter. xml2::as_list(extracted_xml) |> str(max.level = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` #> List of 1 #> $ root:List of 8 #> ..$ address :List of 4 #> ..$ age :List of 1 #> ..$ children : list() #> ..$ firstName :List of 1 #> ..$ isAlive :List of 1 #> ..$ lastName :List of 1 #> ..$ phoneNumbers:List of 2 #> ..$ spouse : list() #> .. ..- attr(*, "null")= chr "true" ``` ::: ::: ## HTML responses **H**yper**T**ext **M**arkup **L**anguage - `text/html`, rarely `application/xhtml+xml` - `httr2::resp_body_html()` uses `xml2::read_html()` - which uses `xml2::read_xml(..., as_html = TRUE)` ::: notes - HTML came before XML, but XML now includes HTML, so - HTML is XML with specific, defined tags - Much more on this in rvest chapter ::: # Binary objects |MIME type |`*` examples |Package | |------------------|-------------------------|-----------| |`image/*` |`png`, `jpeg`, `svg+xml` |`{magick}` | |`audio/*` |`mpeg`, `wav`, `ogg` |`{av}` ? | |`video/*` |`mpeg`, `mp4`, `ogg` |`{av}` ? | |`application/*` |`octet-stream` (catch-all), `x-bzip`, `pdf` |(various) | ## Images ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} resp_body_raw(resp) |> magick::image_read() ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # requires {rsvg} resp_body_raw(resp) |> magick::image_read_svg() ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # requires {pdftools} resp_body_raw(resp) |> magick::image_read_pdf() ``` ::: ::: ::: notes - Can also save directly, but this reads the object directly into R. - SVG and PDF work best with dedicated packages. - Parsing PDFs could be a book itself. ::: ## Video I haven't found anything yet for working with "raw" audio/video! ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} resp_body_raw(resp) |> writeBin(path) ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} av::av_video_convert(path, output = "output.mp4", verbose = TRUE) ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ffmpeg_cmd <- glue::glue( "ffmpeg -v quiet ", "-i {input_path} ", "-ss {start_time} -to {end_time} ", "-c copy {output_path}" ) system(ffmpeg_cmd, ignore.stdout = TRUE) ``` ::: ::: ::: notes - As far as I can tell, you always have to save audio/video, and THEN parse it. - How much do people want here? Pointers in right direction? Full examples (which might not apply in their use case)? - Has anybody worked with video? This feels hacky! ::: ## Audio I haven't found anything yet for working with "raw" audio/video! ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} resp_body_raw(resp) |> writeBin(filename) ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} av::av_audio_convert( path, output = "output.mp3", format = NULL, channels = NULL, sample_rate = NULL, start_time = NULL, total_time = NULL, verbose = TRUE ) ``` ::: ::: ::: notes - How much do people want here? Pointers in right direction? Full examples (which might not apply in their use case)? - Has anybody worked with audio? This feels hacky! ::: ## Raw data Danger zone! ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} resp_body_raw(resp) |> writeBin(filename) ``` ::: ::: ::: notes - Anything else likely has to be saved before any tools will know what to do. - Don't save random binary files if you don't have reason to trust what they are! ::: ## Base64-encoded JSON data - Base64 transforms binary data into text - 6-bit blocks ➡️ 1 of 64 characters ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} raw_data <- resp_body_json(resp) |> _$b64_json |> # Or whatever the element is named jsonlite::base64_dec() magick::image_read(raw_data) # Etc ``` ::: ::: ::: notes - OpenAI API can send generated images this way (that's how I was reminded about it). - 64 = 2^6 ::: # HTTP errors ## HTTP status codes |Range |Description |Notes | |------|--------------|--------------------| |1xx |Informational |Handled by `{curl}` | |2xx |Successful |`resp_*()` | |3xx |Redirection |Auto-followed | |4xx |Client error |"Your fault" | |5xx |Server error |"Server's fault" | ::: notes - As with everything, codes can be incorrectly implemented - "Client error" might result from mistake on server side - "Server error" might mean you sent an expired key ::: ## Handling errors `req_error(req, is_error = NULL, body = NULL)` - `is_error` = function to identify errors - `body` = function to turn error `resp` into message ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Never trigger R errors req |> req_error(is_error = \(resp) FALSE) ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Only trigger R errors for "Server error" responses req |> req_error(is_error = \(resp) resp_status(resp) >= 500) ``` ::: ::: ::: fragment ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Include information from response in error message req |> req_error( body = function(resp) { resp_body_json(resp)$error_msg # Often more complicated than this } ) ``` ::: ::: ::: notes - Add `req_error()` before `req_perform()` (or `req_perform_iterative()`) - Most common use cases: - Don't let httr2 handle errors, so you can do something yourself. - Override bad status codes from server. - Provide more details in error message from server. - TODO: Find an API that returns error details! ::: ## Errors and pagination - `req_perform_iterative()` has `on_error = c("stop", "return")` - On *R* error - "stop" = "throw an R error if any call throws an R error" - "return" = "stop iterating, return everything so far" - `req_error(is_error = \(resp) FALSE)` can cause that not to trigger - May want to combine these for your use-case ## More on retries ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} httr2::req_retry( req, max_tries = NULL, max_seconds = NULL, is_transient = NULL, backoff = NULL, after = NULL ) ``` ::: - `is_transient` = function to decide whether to retry from `resp` - `backoff` = function to convert `tries` to `wait_seconds` - `after` = function to convert `resp` to `wait_seconds` ::: notes - In pagination chapter, we just set max_tries here to 3 - The LinkedIn API throws a transient 404 randomly during some calls, so I set it up to retry on 404. - ::: # Questions?

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
